speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Thursday, March 27, 1997. I'm here with survivor Fred Katz, K-A-T-Z. My name is Lucy Samorodin. We are doing the interview in Baltimore, Maryland, and the interview is being conducted in English. Today is Thursday, March, 27. I'm here with Mr. Fred Katz in Baltimore, Maryland. We are doing the interview in English. My name is Lucy Samorodin. I'm the interviewer. Good morning. Dr. Katz, can you spell your name and tell me the date of birth and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am Fred Katz, and my last name is K-A-T-Z. I was born in Oberlauringen, which is a small village in northern Bavaria in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Oberlauringen?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-B-E-R-L-A-U-R-I-N-G-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near city of Schweinfurt, which is on the Main River, which was made famous during the Second World War because it was the center of the German ball bearing industry. So it's about 100 miles east of Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name was the same. The first one was Manfred, Manfred Emil Katz. And I eventually shortened it to Fred because I was always called Fred, even in Germany. So it wasn't really a change in my mind, but legally, I changed it to Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you paint an early picture of your life when you lived in Oberlauringen? Well, I suppose it's the life of a little child looking up at the world, and the world around me was a, a farming village where there were about 300 to 400 people. I would say about 35 Jewish people, that means about 15 families. The village was, as I see it now, very isolated from the rest of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a sense, each, each individual family had a trade. Most of the people were farmers, particularly the Christians. But there were also one or two Jewish families that were farmers. And then each family had a trade. My father was a butcher. Our neighbor was a baker. And some-- there were two stores owned by Jews. So the, the feeling and the sense I have about the village is it was an insulated little world. By present day standards, it was very different. We didn't have running water. We did have electricity. We did not have phones. There was one telephone in the village, and that was in the post office. There was a post office. There was no such thing as a police. We had no police in, in the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but what I also think of is that there was a Jewish world and the non-Jewish world. The non-Jewish world included going to school in the public school, in the grade school. The Jewish world included the synagogue and going to Hebrew school and the associa-- the intimate associations were mostly with Jews. So as I saw it from the early years is really two worlds.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I should also emphasize is I was born in 1927, which means I started school in 1933 when Hitler came to power. So my earliest memories include the going to school, and that is a picture of pain and horror-- being beaten up going to school every day, dreading to go to school. And what pervades the, the atmosphere for me is one of fear. The first report I got at the end of my first year of school was a one sentence description of me, and it said Fred is scared. Fred ist angstlich. Scared is perhaps not the best translation, but it's the closest thing to the word angstlich. So I was a scared person, and even the teacher picked that up as being the characteristic that stood out for her. It was a women teacher. The teaches, incidentally, did not mistreat us. They were, I would say, quite fair in their conduct of the school in terms of Jewish issues. But for me, the being Jewish was central in terms of that the Christian children seemed to feel they have a right and a duty to beat me up every day. So that's sort of the picture I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your household. Who lived in your house with you, and what was your house like? Can you describe your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The out-- it was a large house by standards that I remember at that time, but also the fact-- I went back in 1974 and looked at-- '74-- '79, I believe it was-- and looked at again. It was still large. Anyway, it was a two story house of-- the living quarters were two stories. And then there was an attic and a cellar, and all of these were in use. The right side of our house on the first floor was a butcher shop. The outside of the house was stucco over stone. It was quite spacious. I think there were four bedrooms on the second floor. And the attic is a place where I played, so it was also in use. And I think there was a maid's room where in the early years, we had a maid who lived there. And the cellar was in use as a storage place for meat. Remember, we had no-- my father was a butcher, and we had no refrigeration facilities. So the cellar was used to store meat, so it was quite large and spacious. But the butcher part was separated from the family quarter. The people who lived there were my parents, and I had a bro-- a half brother and a sister. The sister was 3 and 1/2 years older than I, and my, my half brother was seven years older. When I say half brother, my father was married twice, and the first-- his first wife died while giving birth to my brother, Ludwig, in 1920. So these are the people who lived there. I don't remember anybody, any other relatives who were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the neighbors, your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we lived next to the baker, the Jewish baker. And really, we were sort of on the main st-- I say sort of. What goes for the main street was our street. There were two other streets, but we lived on the main street, number 57, across from the-- on one side across from us was the synagogue. Adjacent to us was the baker. And one of the things I remember with fear and trepidation was that the, the hearse that was used for Jewish funerals was stored behind us, in a house behind us. So I would have nightmares about death and, and-- but also this additional element that we were Kohens-- Katz is Kohen-- which meant Kohens are not supposed to be in contact with the dead. And so here I was living next to the hearse, but strictly forbidden to be in a house where there was death. And so that added to the sense of apprehension and fear about-- dread about death.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway the village was spread out, and, and the life of the people was-- the farmers, particularly, within sort of the European sense, they would live in a village and go out to their farms. It was not like an American farming village where the farmers lived on their farms. So it was a close-- geographically, a close-knit situation of people living together, and Jews and Christians lived next to each other. Did they live in harmony at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, cool harmony, that they lived in cool harmony. There were-- as far as I know, there were no open antagonisms between Jews and Christians in the village. And that in the early years, there probably was a fair amount of cooperation. But in the mid '30s, I think it became increasingly cool, the relationships.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any specific incidents about any cool relationships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, shortly after Hitler came to power-- I would estimate was around 1934-- all the windows in our house were suddenly smashed. I think it was during a night. We had no idea who did it, what happened. And my-- as I mentioned before, we didn't have police in the village. But my father went to the, the regional police, and it was sort of described as the work of Nazi hooligans, but not local. So my father was advised to leave the village for a while, and he did for some weeks and went to the, the small town where he was born, probably about 100 miles away. And after some weeks, he came back. But first of all, we never found out who did it. But basically, the authorities in that early stage were on our side. They were trying to protect us, whereas the local-- the people in the community were stiffly silent. There was no sense that they had any part in it or wanted to be at all a part of it in it. So the assumption was these were outside Nazi hooligans who were beating up on a Jewish house. As far as I know, ours was the only one at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your father and his business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a butcher. And he was a rather distant-- emotionally, a rather distant person. He-- it was not easy for him to-- he was not very verbal, whereas my mother was a very outgoing, warm, verbal person and also, emotionally, a hugging person. My father was not. He was rather distant and inward. Although many years later-- jumping forward here for a second-- many years later, a relative told me that he made the best san-- best sausages, but he also read Shakespeare. So there must be a little more to him than I always thought. But my memory of him were very mixed because due to the Nazi situation, he lost his livelihood. And well, as you may or may not know, one of the first things the Nazis did was to hib-- prohibit the Jewish way of killing meat, the kosher killing of meat. This meant my father's livelihood was gone in one swoop. So but as I saw it, he was this ineffectual man who wasn't feeding his family. He wasn't providing a livelihood, and I was very angry at him. And I expressed anger in many ways. I remember his smoking a pipe, and I was furious that he would spend money buying tobacco and smoking, puffing away at his pipe while we were-- all of us were very aware that we didn't have money to buy food. So I was angry at him, and he was just this distant man not responding. And so that's sort of how I remember him at-- from that time. But also with a fair amount of guilt from my part now that I was angry at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I already touched on her. She was a very warm, outgoing person. She was the sort of person who if anybody in the community was sick, she came running and was forever helping. But she was not neglecting her family. She was emotionally with us for all of us, but also open and with us with people in need wherever they were. So she was of a outgoing, nurturing person. But she was also intelligent and earthy in a way. Essentially, she saved my life. I'm convinced if it had not been for her, I would not be here, that she made the arrangements for rescuing me, for having me be rescued.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Max Katz and my mother's name was Shenny, S-H-E-N-N-Y. It's the best spelling I can think of. I don't know if it's accurate, but I think it is. And so they came from very-- from orthodox families. Well, you asked about my father and my mother. My father and mother were like all the Jews in the community. They were Orthodox. All the Jews were orthodox, but I felt that my father wasn't really all that committed to orthodoxy. But he went along with it. My mother was much more committed, I think, although she was not a crazy orthodox person, not a zealot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your brothers-- your brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my sister and I don't have much of a sense of her. She left about two years before I left. She left in 1937, I believe, to go to the United States where we managed-- my parents managed to get relatives of ours in Ohio to sponsor her. I don't remember any real interaction with her. By contrast, my brother stands out as a beacon of love. He was a most loving person. He was seven years older than I. He gave me presents, and he was simply a very loving person. But there's-- his story, in a sense, is by far the saddest in my family-- that is, his mother died when he was born. And he finally died in the Holocaust. In the in-between, he and my father had a horrible relationship. At some level, my father apparently blamed him for his wife's death. He was not a sophisticated man, but he was forever being furious at my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have an speci--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there was-- there were one or two instances-- my mother always stood up for my brother. But there were one or two instances where my brother had to leave home to stay with relatives because my father was so furiously angry at him. And he-- my brother repaid everything with love. He was the most loving person.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother then eventually left to try to go what was then Palestine in 1930-- early '39. And he signed up with a movement called the Hakhshara, which was a movement to train young men and women to become farmers in Palestine. And they would be trained to get-- to have these practical occupations before they left Germany, before they went to Palestine. So some went to Sweden to learn farming. My brother was sent to Holland to learn farming. And so he went to Holland, and unfortunately, he never got out of Holland. He was captured by the Germans and sent to his death in Poland. Tell me how you celebrated the Jewish holidays, and if you had a favorite holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much in the way of celebration at all. I, I remember we, we did celebrate Friday evening lighting candles, my mother lighting candles. But I don't have a feeling of celebration at all. I remember going to synagogues across the street and standing next to my father. And, and I remember the exact place we stood. And there was one instance where the Nazis shot through the window. And I somehow had some-- which in retrospect sounds like sort of a mystical experience. Namely, I was-- we were next to the window or just one seat over from a window. And at some point, I moved away for no apparent reason, and then they shot through that window while a service was going on. I don't know whether it would have hit me or not. But I-- so anyway, the synagogue was, in one sense, a profound meeting place for the Jewish people of the village. Namely, we had fixed seats where men and women were separate. The women were upstairs, and the men downstairs. So we had-- so I would see where my mother's place was, and I still know exactly where her place was, where my place was, where my father's place was. I also remember where the various other Jews of the community s-- sat. And I remember how they said their prayers, how they sang the songs. And, and so in a sense, there was a vitality there of being alive in the synagogue as Jews that I remember. Now we had no rabbi. We had a teacher who was sort of-- he led the services, but also taught Hebrew classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any specific incidents about this teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, y-- you may have caught my sort of being a little bit anxious on that one. I hated this man with a passion-- that is, he would-- he would beat us when he had arguments with his wife. He would take it out on us. I'm talking about Hebrew classes on Sundays. He was a very difficult person who I think of as having three left feet. I mean, he actually was somewhat deformed, but that wasn't the worst of his deformity. So anyway, I f-- I was very, very scared of him that he threw temper tantrums at us when it was clear he was having problems with his wife. And he hit us with sticks. And so anyway but the, the, the other point is that I wished him dead many a time. But then what sticks with me is that he was finally killed in the Holocaust and leaving me with this particular baggage of guilt about I know I didn't cause him to be killed, but I did wish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother making any special preparations for the holiday, for any holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I do remember for Passover we had a separate set of dishes and even wooden covers for the-- for the tables that were brought out before Passover. And this-- of course, our interview right now is just before Passover, so this is appropriate. We had separate dishes and everything-- separate knives and forks for Passover which were stored in the attic. I remember where they were stored, and they were dragged out after the enormous house cleaning and so on. So in that sense, we were quite traditionally orthodox. I also remember one trauma-- that is during a service in our synagogue, my father once dropped the Torah. It fell on the ground. That was a major calamity. The rabbi had to be called. I mean, we had no rabbi. The rabbi from the region had to be called as to how can the community redeem itself from this horrible sin. And I thought my father thought it sort of a bemused-- he was bemused by it. He wasn't amused by it, but he was a bit bemused. He wasn't so shaken by it, but the rest of the community, it brought a horrible blemish on our whole family that they could commit such sacrilege. I mean, it's like, I don't know, killing somebody. Whatever religious, it was a horrible sin. Well, another piece of horror that comes to my mind in regard to that and my father is that, you know, my father was a butcher, and he supplied the kosher meat. And it was illegal to produce kosher meat, so my father at times cheated. He pretended to get a shipment of meat from France or wherever and sold it when actually it wasn't killed the kosher way. He just got it the ordinary way and, and sold it to the Jewish people as kosher meat. Well, I thought this was the most horrible sin a person can commit. At that time, I, I thought this was just beneath contempt and just reinforced the horrible thoughts I had about my father. As I think about it now, he was feeding his family and had some dignity about it in knowing what, what is right and what is not right, the w-- that his values were on target. But I didn't recognize it at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me your school life, your early school life? How did you get to school? Who were your friends in school? What subjects you liked or didn't like? And what our school room looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a one room school room for, for the lower grade. There were two rooms, essentially. One was for the lower grades, and one was for the upper grades. And the lower grades were, I would say, through grades four, if I-- roughly. I don't recall the physical part of the layout in the room except that there were, perhaps, 30 or so children for all these grades in that one room and a very cool looking, elegantly dressed teacher. Going to school was simply a nightmare. That, I remember very vividly, that I dreaded going to school because I was being beaten up on the way to school and on the way out of school. And in between, I was expected to help my schoolmates with their homework. So it was-- dread is perhaps an understatement. I dreaded to leave house to go to school. In school, the teachers were fair, as I mentioned before. They did not openly discriminate, despite the fact that the senior teacher-- that is the teacher for the upper classes-- was also the local Nazi party official for the region. But to my knowledge, in class, that was never brought into play-- that is, that they were fair. Except I do remember in class when children tortured me or, or were nasty to me. And they poked things at me from behind my chair. And if I then moved too suddenly, the teacher would call on me and slap me because I shouldn't have moved, and I should have been quiet. She didn't know what had happened, and I didn't dare tell her what had happened. So I remember to this day having to come before the class and she would spank my hand with a stick. It still hurts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you tell your parents about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To my knowledge, I never told my parents. I don't reme-- I might have, but I do not recall ever talking to my parents about my fears of going to school or about being beaten up. I don't recall talking about it, but I am also convinced that my parents knew. Certainly, my mother had the sensitivity to know. But I don't recall conversations about it with my parents about the daily horror. But the-- gradually, what it did turn into was that I would plead with my parents to let me go, that I wanted to leave.
